questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If the expression \(x^2-11 x+a\) and \(x^2-14 x+2 a\) must have a common factor and \(\mathrm{a} \neq 0\), then, the common factor is
questions.solution:
Here Let \(x-\alpha\) is the common factor then \(\mathrm{x}=\alpha\) is root of the corresponding equation \(\therefore \alpha^2-11 \alpha+a=0\)
\(\alpha^2-14 \alpha+2 \mathrm{a}=0\)
Subtracting \(3 \alpha-a=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=a / 3\)
Hence \(\frac{a^2}{9}-11 \frac{a}{3}+a=0, a=0\) or \(a=24\) since \(\mathrm{a} \neq 0, \mathrm{a}=24\)
\(\therefore\) the common factor of \(\left\{\begin{array}{l}x^2-11 x+24 \\ x^2-14 x+48\end{array}\right.\) is clearly \(x -8\)
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the roots of the equation $x^3-6 x^2+11 x-6=0$ and if $a=\alpha^2+\beta^2+\gamma^2$, $b=\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha$ and $c=(\alpha+\beta)(\beta+\gamma)(\gamma+\alpha)$, then the correct inequality among the following is
questions.solution: Given equation $x^3-6 x^2+11 x-6=0$ has the roots $\alpha, \beta \gamma$. Given, $\quad a=\alpha^2+\beta^2+\gamma^2$ ...(i) $b=\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha$ ...(ii) $c=(\alpha+\beta)(\beta+\gamma)(\gamma+\alpha)$ ...(iii) In cubic equation the sum of the roots $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=-\left(\frac{-6}{1}\right)=6$ $\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=\left(\frac{11}{1}\right)=11$ product of the roots $\alpha \cdot \beta \cdot \gamma=-\left(\frac{-6}{1}\right)=6$ From Eq. (ii), $b=11$ From Eq. (i), $a=\alpha^2+\beta^2+\gamma^2$ $\Rightarrow \quad a=(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)^2-2(\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha)$ $\Rightarrow \quad a=(6)^2-2(11) \Rightarrow 36-22$ $\Rightarrow \quad a=14$ From Eq. (iii) $c=(\alpha+\beta)(\beta+\gamma)(\gamma+\alpha)$ $=\left(\alpha \beta+\beta^2+\alpha \gamma+\beta \gamma\right)(\gamma+\alpha)$ $=\alpha \beta \gamma+\beta^2 \gamma+\alpha \gamma^2+\beta \gamma^2+\alpha^2 \beta$ $+\alpha \beta^2+\alpha^2 \gamma+\alpha \beta \gamma$ $\Rightarrow c=[(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)-\gamma][(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)-\alpha]$ $[(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)-\beta]$ $=(6-\gamma)(6-\alpha)(6-\beta)$ $=(36-6 \gamma-6 \alpha+\alpha \gamma)(6-\beta)$ $=216-36 \gamma-36 \alpha+6 \alpha \gamma-36 \beta+6 \gamma \beta$ $+6 \alpha \beta-\alpha \beta \gamma$ $=216-\alpha \beta \gamma+6(\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha)-36(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)$ $=216-6+6(11)-36(6)$ $=210+66-216=60$ Hence, $\quad c=60$ $\Rightarrow \quad b < a < c$Alternate method Given equation is $x^3-6 x^2+11 x-6=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad(x-1)(x-2)(x-3)=0$ The roots of this equation are 1,2 , and 3 . Let $\alpha=1, \beta=2, \gamma=3$ Now, $\quad a=\alpha^2+\beta^2+\gamma^2$ $=1+4+9=14$ $b=\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha$ $=2+6+3=11$ $c=(\alpha+\beta)(\beta+\gamma)(\gamma+\alpha)$ $=3 \cdot 5 \cdot 4=60$ From the values of $a, b$ and $c$ it is clear that $b < a < c$.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.q: If <math><mi>α</mi></math> and <math><mi> </mi><mi>β</mi></math> are roots of the equation <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> then the value of <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">tan</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">tan</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>β</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced></math> is
questions.solution:
x2+5x-6=0
x2+5x-6=0
x2+6x-x-6=0
xx+6-1x+6=0
x-1x+6=0
x=1, nbsp;x≠-6 (Since modulus can not be giving negative values)
∴  x=1
∴  x=± 1
α=1,β=-1
∴   tan-1⁡α-tan-1⁡β=tan-1⁡1-tan-1⁡-1
=π4--π4
=π2 = π 2

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If the equations $x^2+a x+b=0$ and $x^2+b x+a=0(a \neq b)$ have a common root, then $a+b$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $\alpha$ be the common root, then
$$
\begin{array}{rlrl} 
& \alpha^2+a \alpha+b & =0 \\
\text { and } & \alpha^2+b \alpha+a & =0 \\
\therefore & & \frac{\alpha^2}{a^2-b^2} & =\frac{\alpha}{b-a}=\frac{1}{b-a} \\
\Rightarrow & & \frac{\alpha}{b-a} & =\frac{1}{b-a} \Rightarrow \alpha=1 \\
\text { Now, } \quad & \frac{\alpha^2}{a^2-b^2} & =\frac{\alpha}{b-a} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{(a+b)(a-b)} & =\frac{1}{-(a-b)} \\
\Rightarrow & a+b & =-1
\end{array}
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.q: If <math><msup><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> then value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
4x-3x-12=3x+12 -22x-1

4x+22x-1=3x+12+3x-12

4x+4x2=3x3+3x3

⇒ 4x1+12=3x3+13

⇒  43x=23×43=833=23123=4332

⇒ x=32
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: For what values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>Z</mi></math>, the quadratic expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1991</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></math> can be factorised as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>Z</mi></math>?
questions.solution:
Since, he quadratic expression x+ax+1991+1 can be factorised as x+bx+c, therefore

x+ax+1991+1=x+bx+c

This implies that x+ax+1991+1 has roots -b & -c i.e., integer roots. So, discriminant of  x2+1991+ax+1991a+1=0must be perfect square.

1991+a2-41991a+1=m2

⇒1991-a2=m2+4

⇒1991-a2-m2=4

⇒1991-a-m1991-a+m=4

Since, both 1991-a-m and 1991-a+m are integers, therefore possible choices are

1991-a-m1991-a+m=1×4

1991-a-m1991-a+m=-1×-4

1991-a-m1991-a+m=2×2

1991-a-m1991-a+m=-2×-2

Only possible result is m=0, so

a=1989, 1993
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> be the roots of equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>α</mi><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>∀</mo><mo> </mo><mi>n</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>a</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>8</mn></msub></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>9</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, α, β are roots of x2-6x-2=0.

⇒ α2-6α-2=0 and  β2-6β-2=0

⇒  α2-2=6α andβ2-2=6β  ...1

a10-2a82a9=α10-β10-2α8-β82α9-β9

=α10-2α8-β10-2β82α9-β9

= α 8 α 2 - 2 - β 8 β 2 - 2 2 α 9 - β 9

= α 8 6 α - β 8 6 β 2 α 9 - β 9        [ ∵  From (1)]

 =6α9-6β92α9-β9=3

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Each of the roots of the equation $x^3-6 x^2+6 x-5=0$ are increased by $h$. So that the new transformed equation does not contain $x^2$ term, then $h$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given equation is
$x^3-6 x^2+6 x-5=0$
$x$ of this equation replaced by $x+h$.
$\begin{gathered}\therefore(x+h)^3-6(x+h)^2+6(x+h)-5=0 \\ x^3+h^3+3 x^2 h+3 x h^2-6\left(x^2+h^2+2 x h\right) \\ +6 x+6 h-5=0\end{gathered}$
Since, coefficient of $x^2=0$
$\therefore \quad 3 h-6=0 \Rightarrow h=2$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are roots of the equation, $x^2-4 \sqrt{2} k x+2 e^{4 \ln k}-1=0$ for some $k$, and $\alpha^2+\beta^2=66$, then $\alpha^3+\beta^3$ is equal to:
questions.solution:
$x^2-4 \sqrt{2} k x+2 e^{4 \ln k}-1=0$ or, $x^2-4 \sqrt{2} k x+2 k^4-1=0$ $\alpha+\beta=4 \sqrt{2} k$ and $\alpha \cdot \beta=2 k^4-1$ Squaring both sides, we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\alpha+\beta)^2=(4 \sqrt{2} k)^2 \\
&\Rightarrow \alpha^2+\beta^2+2 \alpha \beta=32 k^2 \\
&66+2 \alpha \beta=32 k^2 \\
&66+2\left(2 k^4-1\right)=32 k^2 \\
&66+4 k^4-2=32 k^2 \\
&\Rightarrow 4 k^4-32 k^2+64=0 \\
&\text { or, } k^4-8 k^2+16=0 \\
&\Rightarrow\left(k^2\right)^2-8 k^2+16=0 \\
&\Rightarrow\left(k^2-4\right)\left(k^2-4\right)=0 \\
&\Rightarrow k^2=4, k^2=4 \\
&\Rightarrow k=\pm 2
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, $\alpha^3+\beta^3=(\alpha+\beta)\left(\alpha^2+\beta^2-\alpha \beta\right)$
$$
\therefore \alpha^3+\beta^3=(4 \sqrt{2} k)\left[66-\left(2 k^4-1\right)\right]
$$
Putting $k=-2,(k=+2$ cannot be taken because it does not satisfy the above equation)
$\therefore \alpha^3+\beta^3=(4 \sqrt{2}(-2))\left[66-2(-2)^4-1\right]$
$\alpha^3+\beta^3=(-8 \sqrt{2})(66-32+1)=$
$(-8 \sqrt{2})(35)$
$\therefore \alpha^3+\beta^3=-280 \sqrt{2}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo> </mo><mo>∈ </mo><mi>R</mi><mo> </mo></math> and the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mo> </mo><mo>[</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>]</mo><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mo> </mo><mo>[</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>]</mo><mo>)</mo><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></math> (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>]</mo></math> denotes the greatest integer <math> <mrow> <mo>≤</mo> </mrow> </math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi></math>) has no integral solution, then all possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> lie in the interval
options: <math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mo></mo><mo>-</mo><mo>∞</mo> <mtext>,</mtext> <mo>-</mo> <mn>2</mn> </mrow> </mfenced> <mo>∪</mo> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> <mtext>,</mtext> <mo>∞</mo> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math>

<math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> <mtext>, </mtext> <mn>0</mn> </mrow> </mfenced> <mo>∪</mo> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mn>0</mn> <mtext>, </mtext> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math>
questions.solution:
Let    x-[x]=t

∴   -3t2+2t+a2=0

As x is not an integer, t≠0     ∴  a≠0

For a root of x to exist at least one of the roots of t should be between 0 and 1.

Roots    =-2±4-4-3a2-6

              =-2±4+12a2-6

              =-1±1+3a2-3=1∓1+3a23

As we observe, one root is surely less than zero,

i.e.,   1-1+3a23

∴    For a solution to exist,

       1+1+3a23<1

∴   1+1+3a2<3

∴   1+3a2<2

∴   1+3a2<4

∴   3a2<3

∴   a2<1

∴   a∈-1,0∪0,1.     ∵   a≠0
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.q: The number of real roots of the equation, <math><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is:
questions.solution:
Let ex=t∈0,∞
Given equation
t4+t3-4t2+t+1=0
t2+t-4+1t+1t2=0
t2+1t2+t+1t-4=0
Let t+1t=α
α2-2+α-4=0
α2+α-6=0
α2+α-6=0
α=-3,2⇒α=2⇒ex+e-x=2
x=0 only solutions

questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Let <math><mi>S</mi></math>, be the set of all real roots of the equation, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><msup><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then
questions.solution:
Let 3x=t
tt-1+2=t-1+t-2
t2-t+2=t-1+t-2

Let, ft=t2-t+2 & gt=t-1+t-2

ft & gt has a solution at t=a.
3x=a ⇒x=log3⁡a.
So, it is a singleton set.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If one root of the equation \((l-\mathrm{m}) \mathrm{x}^2+l \mathrm{x}+1=0\) is double of the other and if \(l\) is real then the greatest value of \(\mathrm{m}\) is \((l \neq \mathrm{m})\) :
questions.solution:
Let the roots of the equation be \(\alpha\) and \(2 \alpha\), then
\(\alpha+2 \alpha=\frac{l}{\mathrm{~m}-l} \text { and } \alpha \cdot 2 \alpha=\frac{1}{l-\mathrm{m}}\)
\(\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{l}{3(\mathrm{~m}-l)} \text { and } \alpha^2=\frac{1}{2(l-\mathrm{m})} \text {. }\)
Eliminating \(\alpha\), we get
\(\frac{l^2}{9(\mathrm{~m}-l)^2}=\frac{1}{2(l-\mathrm{m})} \Rightarrow 2 l^2-9 l+9 \mathrm{~m}=0\)
Since \(l\) is real, so \((-9)^2-4 \cdot 2 \cdot 9 \mathrm{~m} \geq 0\)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{m} \leq \frac{9}{8}\)
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
Let $a, b, c, p, q$ be real numbers.
Suppose, $\alpha, \beta$ are the roots of the equation $x^2+2 p x+q=0$ and $\alpha, \frac{1}{\beta}$ are the roots of the equation $a x^2+2 b x+c=0$, where $\beta^2 \notin\{-1,0,1\}$.
Statement $1\left(p^2-q\right)\left(b^2-a c\right) \geq 0$.
Statement $2 b \neq p a$ or $c \neq q a$.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is a correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not a correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true
questions.solution:
Given, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots of $x^2+2 p x+q=0$.
$$
\begin{array}{rlrl} 
& \therefore & \alpha+\beta & =-2 p \\
\text { and } & \alpha \beta & =q
\end{array}
$$
$\alpha$ and $\frac{1}{\beta}$ are the roots of $a x^2+2 b x+c=0$
and
$$
\alpha+\frac{1}{\beta}=\frac{-2 b}{a}
$$

and
$$
\frac{\alpha}{\beta}=\frac{c}{a}
$$
$$
\text { Now, } \begin{aligned}
\left(p^2-q\right)\left(b^2-a c\right) & =\left[\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{-2}\right)^2-\alpha \beta\right]\left[\left(\frac{\alpha+\frac{1}{\beta}}{2}\right)^2-\frac{\alpha}{\beta}\right] a^2 \\
& =\frac{(\alpha-\beta)^2}{16}\left(\alpha-\frac{1}{\beta}\right)^2 \cdot a^2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$
Statement 1 is true.
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\text { Again now, } & p a=-\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right) a=-\frac{a}{2}(\alpha+\beta) \\
\text { and } & b=-\frac{a}{2}\left(\alpha+\frac{1}{\beta}\right) \\
& p a \neq b \Rightarrow \alpha+\frac{1}{\beta} \neq \alpha+\beta \\
\Rightarrow & \beta \neq 1 \\
\because & \beta^2 \neq\{-1,0,1\}, \text { correct. } \\
\text { Similarly, if } & c \neq q x \\
\Rightarrow & a \frac{\alpha}{\beta} \neq a \alpha \beta \Rightarrow \alpha\left(\beta-\frac{1}{\beta}\right) \neq 0
\end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & \alpha \neq 0 \text { and } \beta-\frac{1}{\beta} \neq 0 \\
\Rightarrow & \beta \neq\{-1,0,1\}
\end{array}
$$
Statement 2 is true.
Both Statement 1 and Statement 2 are true. But Statement 2 does not explain Statement 1.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.q: Find the maximum and minimum values of <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <msup> <mi>x</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <mo>-</mo> <mi>x</mi> <mo>+</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <msup> <mi>x</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <mo>+</mo> <mi>x</mi> <mo>+</mo> <mn>1</mn>  </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math> for real values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
questions.solution:
Let x 2 - x + 1 x 2 + x + 1 = y ⇒ x 2 + x + 1 y= x 2 - x + 1
⇒  y - 1 x 2 + y + 1 x + y - 1 = 0. On Solving for x, we get
x = - y + 1 ± y + 1 2 - 4 y - 1 2 2 y - 1  = - y + 1 2 y - 1 ± 1 2 y - 1 - 3 y - 3 y - 1 / 3

Now for real values of x we will get real values of y, hence x to be real - 3 y - 3 y - 1 / 3 ≥ 0 ⇒ y ∈ 1 3 ,⁡ 3 .
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If the roots of the quadratic equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> are  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mi>k</mi></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
We have k+1k+k+2k+1=-ba ....(i)

and k+1k·k+2k+1=ca

⇒k+2k=ca

or  2k=ca-1=c-aa

or k=2ac-a .....(ii)

Now eliminate k putting the value of k in 1st  relation, we get

c+a2a+2cc+a=-ba

⇒(c+a)2+4ac=-2b(a+c)

⇒(a+c)2+2b(a+c)=-4ac

Adding b2 on both sides,

(a+b+c)2=b2-4ac
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
